questions.question: A Spark job over ADLS Gen2 is slow even though the dataset is only 50 GB. Investigation reveals 1.2 million tiny CSV files. What is the most effective remediation?
options: Switch the storage account to LRS replication
Compact the small files into larger Parquet files (~256 MB-1 GB) using a job before processing
Move the data to Azure Files instead of ADLS Gen2
Disable the hierarchical namespace
questions.explanation: The small-files problem is the classic ADLS performance killer — listing and opening millions of tiny files dominates runtime. Compacting them into larger Parquet (or Delta) files restores performance. Replication and disabling HNS don't address the file-count cost; Azure Files is not a big-data store.

questions.question: A daily fact table is queried almost exclusively by `eventDate` and produces ~100 GB/day. Which partition design is best for query performance?

Partition by `eventDate` (one folder per day)
Partition by `userId` (millions of folders)
Partition by `randomGuid` to distribute writes
questions.explanation: Partitioning by `eventDate` matches the dominant filter and yields ~100 GB partitions — within the recommended 1-10 GB range when split across files. No partitioning loses pruning; user-id or random-guid partitioning creates millions of tiny partitions and destroys performance.

No IR is required for SQL Server

Enable CDC on the SQL database and stream changes through Event Hubs into a Delta sink, with idempotent MERGE upserts

questions.question: A large enterprise wants policy and RBAC inheritance to apply uniformly to all production subscriptions but not to dev/test. Which Azure construct is the right anchor?

A Management Group hierarchy with separate Prod and Non-Prod groups
Resource Groups split by environment
A second Microsoft Entra tenant for production
questions.explanation: Management Groups apply Azure Policy and RBAC down through their subscriptions, so a Prod MG can carry strict policies while a Non-Prod MG carries looser ones. Resource Groups don't span subscriptions; a second tenant adds heavy identity overhead unrelated to policy.

Azure Landing Zones with automated subscription vending and CAF reference architecture
Single shared subscription with resource groups per team
Separate Microsoft Entra tenants per application

questions.question: A new IoT platform ingests one million sensor readings per second and runs ad-hoc analytical queries on time-windowed slices. Which Azure data store is the best primary fit?

Azure Data Explorer (Kusto)
Azure Cache for Redis as system of record
Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
questions.explanation: Azure Data Explorer is purpose-built for high-ingest time-series telemetry with fast ad-hoc analytical queries. Azure SQL and PostgreSQL are OLTP engines that struggle with that ingest rate; Redis is a cache, not a system of record.

questions.question: A team migrating SQL Server to Azure SQL Managed Instance must keep downtime under 5 minutes. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Backup-and-restore with a maintenance window of several hours
Azure Database Migration Service in online (continuous) mode with cut-over after CDC catches up
Manual export/import using BCP
Direct disk copy of the SQL Server data files
questions.explanation: DMS online mode performs an initial seed and continuously replicates changes via CDC, allowing a near-zero downtime cut-over. Offline backup/restore requires a long window; BCP and disk copy don't sustain low-downtime.

questions.question: A 6-engineer team is starting a new SaaS product. They have no platform team, no service mesh expertise, and tight timelines. Which architecture is most likely to succeed first?
options: 20-service microservices on AKS with Istio from day one
A modular monolith deployed to App Service or Container Apps
A serverless functions mesh with no shared database
Three separate monoliths owned by no one in particular
questions.explanation: A 6-engineer team without platform support pays a heavy operational tax for microservices. A modular monolith delivers value faster and preserves the option to extract services later. Service-mesh microservices and a serverless mesh are over-engineered for the situation.

questions.question: An order service must publish an `OrderPlaced` event reliably whenever it commits a new order, even if the message broker is briefly unavailable. Which pattern best meets the requirement?
options: Two-phase commit between the database and the broker
Outbox pattern: persist the event to a local outbox table in the same DB transaction; a dispatcher publishes it
Fire-and-forget publish before persisting the order
Synchronously block the order API until the broker returns success
questions.explanation: The outbox pattern guarantees the event survives even when the broker is down: it is committed atomically with the order, and a dispatcher publishes asynchronously with retries. 2PC is brittle and rarely supported by modern brokers; fire-and-forget loses messages; synchronous publish couples availability.

citations.snippet: Section on Event-Driven Designs from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A workload requires RTO of under 60 seconds and RPO of zero across two Azure regions. Which architecture meets both?
options: Active/passive with backup/restore on failover
Pilot light DR with a 4-hour redeploy
Active/active across regions with multi-region write database (e.g., Cosmos DB) and global front door
Single-region deployment with daily backups
questions.explanation: Only active/active with a multi-region-write data layer can keep both RTO and RPO near zero. Active/passive and pilot light have minutes-to-hours RTO; single-region cannot survive a regional outage at all.

questions.question: A finance team wants to attribute Azure cost back to the team that owns each resource without manual reconciliation each month. What is the foundational requirement?
options: Move every team into its own subscription, regardless of size
Enforce required tags (costCenter, owner, env) at creation time via Azure Policy and use Cost Management views by tag
Disable Azure Cost Management
Switch all workloads to Spot VMs
questions.explanation: Showback hinges on consistent, enforced tags so Cost Management can split spend by owner/team. Per-team subscriptions help but are not sufficient and are heavyweight. Disabling Cost Management or forcing Spot does not solve attribution.

PaaS using Azure App Service

Replacing failed physical disks in the datacentre
Managing user identities and access permissions

Two VMs in the same Availability Set
Two or more VMs spread across Availability Zones
A single VM with a managed identity

All three resources are permanently deleted along with the resource group
The resources are paused and can be restored within 30 days from a recycle bin
Only resources older than 30 days are deleted; newer ones are preserved
questions.explanation: Deleting a resource group permanently deletes every resource inside it. Azure does not provide a recycle bin for resource groups; that's why the deletion is gated by an explicit confirmation.

Apply an Azure Policy with a Deny effect at the management group scope
Remove all Owner role assignments
Configure a Network Security Group on the storage account

Call the pre-built sentiment endpoint in Azure AI Language
Fine-tune a GPT-4 model with thousands of labelled examples
Build a regular-expression classifier

Groundedness detection in Azure AI Foundry
Network Security Groups on the chatbot's VNet
Azure Reservations for the OpenAI resource

options: Azure OpenAI Standard pay-per-token deployment
Azure OpenAI deployment using Provisioned Throughput Units
Azure Functions consumption plan calling an external API
Azure AI Foundry serverless endpoint without throughput reservation
questions.explanation: PTU reserves dedicated capacity, giving predictable latency under steady load. Standard pay-per-token shares regional capacity and can throttle under spikes. Functions and serverless endpoints don't add latency guarantees.

questions.question: A developer wants the model to always return its answer as a JSON object matching a defined schema, with no surrounding prose. What is the most reliable approach?
options: Ask politely in the prompt: 'Please reply only in JSON.'
Use the API's structured-output / response_format JSON schema feature with a low temperature
Set temperature to 1.0 and rely on randomness
Place the schema description in a separate downstream service
questions.explanation: Structured output with a JSON schema constrains the decoding step itself, which is far more reliable than natural-language instructions. Low temperature reduces variability further. Polite phrasing works most of the time but cannot guarantee the format.

questions.question: A RAG application notices that pure vector search returns semantically related but lexically off-topic results. Which Azure AI Search configuration most improves precision on natural-language queries?
options: Drop vector search and rely solely on BM25
Use hybrid search (keyword + vector) with semantic ranking
Increase the embedding dimension to 4096
Disable the language analyzer
questions.explanation: Hybrid search fuses keyword (BM25) and vector signals via RRF, and semantic ranking re-orders the top results with a deep model — this combination typically beats either keyword-only or vector-only retrieval on natural-language queries.

questions.question: A SaaS team wants the highest deployment frequency with the lowest merge friction and is willing to invest in feature flags. Which branching strategy fits best?

Trunk-based development with short-lived feature branches and feature flags
A single branch with no PRs and direct pushes to main
Long-lived feature branches that integrate quarterly
questions.explanation: Trunk-based development with feature flags maximises integration frequency while keeping unfinished work hidden in production. GitFlow adds ceremony unsuited to SaaS; quarterly integration creates massive merge debt; no-PR pushes break review and traceability.

A workload-identity-federation service connection that exchanges a federated token at runtime
An on-prem agent with hard-coded `az login` credentials
Disable authentication for the deployment step

questions.question: A team running Terraform from multiple parallel pipelines occasionally sees corrupted state. What is the recommended remediation on Azure?
options: Disable state files; let Terraform reconcile every run from scratch
Use the azurerm Storage backend with state locking via blob lease
Commit the state file to Git and rebase frequently
Run all pipelines from a single shared developer laptop
questions.explanation: The azurerm backend on Azure Storage uses blob lease as a state lock so concurrent runs serialise safely. State files must never be committed to Git (they contain sensitive output), and removing state breaks Terraform's diff model.

Canary deployment with progressive traffic shifting
Recreate strategy (delete old, deploy new)
A/B testing in feature-flag UI only — no deployment changes
questions.explanation: Canary deployments shift traffic in small increments and monitor KPIs at each step, exactly matching the requirement. Blue/Green cuts over all-or-nothing; recreate causes downtime; A/B in UI doesn't address build deployment safety.

questions.question: A new pricing engine is shipped behind a feature flag. Customers report failures within minutes of enabling it. What is the fastest, lowest-risk recovery action?
options: Roll back the deployment by redeploying the previous build immediately
Flip the feature flag off so the new code path is no longer executed, then investigate
Take the application offline for 4 hours
Notify customers to clear their browser cache
questions.explanation: The whole point of a feature flag is fast, no-deploy rollback of the new code path. Disabling the flag returns users to the known-good path immediately. Redeploying takes longer and is unnecessary if the flag covers the change.

questions.question: An organisation wants to enforce MFA on all users but is worried a misconfigured policy could lock everyone out. What is the safest deployment approach?

Roll out the policy in report-only mode first, review sign-in logs, then enable
Disable the global admin's MFA before enabling the policy
Apply the policy only to guest users
questions.explanation: Report-only mode logs what the policy would have done without enforcing it, letting admins validate impact and unblock affected users before enabling. Disabling the admin's MFA is exactly the wrong move; the goal is to confirm correctness, not bypass it.

An Azure RBAC custom role
Microsoft Defender for Identity

Use the `AzureFrontDoor.Backend` service tag as the source
Allow `Internet` as the source and rely on Defender for Cloud
Drop the NSG and rely solely on the application gateway

Azure Firewall Premium
Azure DDoS Protection Basic
An NSG with deny-all rules
questions.explanation: Azure Firewall Premium adds TLS inspection and IDPS to Standard's stateful filtering, satisfying both requirements. DDoS protects against L3/L4 floods; NSGs do not perform deep packet inspection.

questions.question: A compliance auditor requires that even an Owner cannot permanently delete a Key Vault secret within 90 days of deletion. What feature should be enabled?
options: Soft delete only
Purge protection (in addition to soft delete)
Key wrapping with HSM
A diagnostic setting to Log Analytics
questions.explanation: Soft delete recovers deleted objects within the retention window, but an Owner can purge them. Purge protection blocks any purge — even by Owners — until the retention period elapses, satisfying the auditor.

citations.snippet: Section on Key Vault and Secrets from the AZ-500 certification guide.

questions.question: A team is told to improve their Microsoft Defender for Cloud Secure Score the most efficiently. Which approach yields the largest score improvement?
options: Disable Defender recommendations they don't like
Remediate recommendations weighted highest in the Secure Score, starting with the controls covering the most resources
Lower the Secure Score weighting in the workspace
Remove all virtual machines from monitoring
questions.explanation: Secure Score is a weighted score per control. Remediating high-weight recommendations that affect many resources moves the score the most. Disabling recommendations or removing resources hides risk rather than reducing it.

citations.snippet: Section on Defender for Cloud Recommendations from the AZ-500 certification guide.

questions.question: An e-commerce team needs to store a product catalog where each product has a different set of attributes (electronics need wattage, books need ISBN, etc.). Which data store best fits?
options: Azure SQL Database with a strict relational schema
Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL using the document model
Azure Cache for Redis as the system of record
Azure Files for direct file storage
questions.explanation: A document database fits a heterogeneous catalog well — each item can carry its own attributes without forcing a wide, sparse relational table. Redis is a cache, not a system of record; Files stores blobs.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Relational vs Non-relational Data from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: A retail company runs nightly BI queries that aggregate two billion sales rows by region. Which Azure service is best suited to host this workload?
options: Azure SQL Database (single database, General Purpose tier)
Azure Synapse Analytics dedicated SQL pool
Azure Cache for Redis
Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
questions.explanation: Synapse dedicated SQL pool is purpose-built for OLAP workloads — column-store, MPP, and partitioning. Azure SQL Database and PostgreSQL are OLTP-oriented; Redis is an in-memory cache.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Data Workloads (OLTP / OLAP) from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: An on-premises SQL Server 2019 application uses SQL Agent jobs, cross-database queries, and CLR assemblies. The team wants to migrate to Azure with the least code change. Which Azure service is the best fit?
options: Azure SQL Database (single database)
SQL Managed Instance
Azure Database for PostgreSQL
Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL
questions.explanation: SQL Managed Instance exposes the full SQL Server instance surface (Agent, cross-database queries, CLR), making it the lowest-friction lift-and-shift target. Azure SQL Database does not support those features at the instance level.

citations.snippet: Section on Azure SQL Family from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: A team is provisioning Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server for a production workload that requires automatic failover within the same region. What should they configure at create time?
options: Burstable SKU with public endpoint
Zone-redundant high availability with a standby in a different Availability Zone
A single read replica in another region
Auto-grow storage with manual scaling only
questions.explanation: Zone-redundant HA places a synchronous standby in a different Availability Zone with automatic failover. Read replicas are async and not a failover target. Burstable is a dev SKU.

citations.snippet: Section on Managed PostgreSQL and MySQL from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: A Cosmos DB container is throttling with 429 responses on a small subset of partition keys while the average RU consumption is well under the provisioned limit. What is the most likely cause?
options: Insufficient storage capacity in the container
A hot partition caused by a low-cardinality partition key
Strong consistency level being too weak
Cosmos DB does not support 429 responses
questions.explanation: RU/s is allocated evenly across logical partitions. A low-cardinality or skewed partition key creates a hot partition that exhausts its share of RU/s and returns 429, even when overall provisioned throughput looks fine.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Cosmos DB Overview from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: A team needs to store 200 TB of nightly backup files that are rarely read after 30 days but must be retained for 7 years for compliance. Which configuration minimises storage cost?
options: Blob Storage in the Hot access tier with GRS replication
Blob Storage with a lifecycle policy: Hot for 30 days, then Archive tier with LRS
Azure Table Storage with row-level encryption
Azure SQL Database with TDE enabled
questions.explanation: Backups that are rarely accessed are a textbook fit for the Archive tier; LRS replication is sufficient for inert backup data. Hot tier is far more expensive for cold data; Table and SQL are not designed for large blob retention.

questions.question: A data engineer wants to run ad-hoc T-SQL queries directly against Parquet files in ADLS Gen2 without provisioning a warehouse or paying for idle compute. Which Synapse capability fits?
options: Dedicated SQL pool
Serverless SQL pool
Apache Spark pool
Synapse Link for Cosmos DB
questions.explanation: Serverless SQL pool charges per TB scanned and queries data-lake files directly with T-SQL — no provisioning required. Dedicated SQL pools require provisioning and incur cost while idle.

citations.snippet: Section on Synapse Analytics Basics from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: A Power BI report against Azure Synapse must reflect every transaction within seconds and the dataset is too large to fit in capacity memory. Which storage mode is most appropriate?
options: Import
DirectQuery
Live connection to a CSV file
Email subscription with a static PDF
questions.explanation: DirectQuery issues live queries to Synapse for every visual, so reports are always fresh and there's no in-memory cap. Import requires periodic refresh and has memory limits; CSV and PDF subscriptions don't satisfy the latency requirement.

questions.question: An organisation wants to enforce Conditional Access policies that block sign-ins from untrusted locations. Which Microsoft Entra licence is the minimum required?
options: Microsoft Entra ID Free
Microsoft Entra ID P1
Microsoft Entra ID P2
No licence is required — the feature is included with any Azure subscription
questions.explanation: Conditional Access requires at least Microsoft Entra ID P1. P2 adds Identity Protection and PIM. The Free tier does not include policy-based access controls.

questions.question: A team needs developers to read and write blobs in one specific storage account but should not be able to delete the account or change its configuration. Which built-in role and scope combination is most appropriate?
options: Owner at the subscription scope
Contributor at the resource group scope
Storage Blob Data Contributor at the storage account scope
Reader at the resource scope
questions.explanation: Storage Blob Data Contributor grants data-plane read/write on blobs without management-plane permissions on the account. Owner/Contributor would let developers delete or reconfigure the account; Reader does not allow writes.

Cool
Cold
Archive

questions.question: A developer shuts down an Azure VM each evening using the OS shutdown command. The next month the bill shows the VM still incurred compute charges overnight. What should they change?
options: Switch the VM disk to Standard HDD
Stop the VM from the Azure portal or CLI so it transitions to 'Stopped (deallocated)'
Add a public IP address to the VM
Resize the VM to a smaller SKU
questions.explanation: An OS-level shutdown leaves the VM in 'Stopped' state where compute is still billed. Stopping from Azure (`az vm deallocate`) transitions to 'Stopped (deallocated)' and stops compute charges.

citations.snippet: Section on VM Lifecycle Management from the AZ-104 certification guide.

questions.question: An admin wants traffic from VMs in a subnet to reach Azure Storage over a private IP rather than the public endpoint. Which feature should they use?
options: Service Endpoint for Microsoft.Storage
Private Endpoint for the storage account
An NSG rule allowing the storage public IP
VNet peering with the storage account
questions.explanation: A Private Endpoint assigns a private IP from the subnet to the storage account, so traffic stays inside the VNet and DNS resolves to that private IP. Service Endpoints route traffic on the Microsoft backbone but the resource keeps a public IP.

questions.question: A web app is hosted in three regions and needs path-based routing, a WAF, and global edge caching. Which Azure service is the best fit?
options: Standard Load Balancer in each region
Azure Front Door
Azure Traffic Manager with priority routing
Application Gateway in a single region
questions.explanation: Azure Front Door is global, supports path-based routing, integrates a WAF, and provides edge caching — covering all three requirements. Traffic Manager is DNS-only (no WAF/caching); Application Gateway is regional only.

citations.snippet: Section on Load Balancers and DNS from the AZ-104 certification guide.

questions.question: An admin needs to alert when CPU% exceeds 85% on any of 200 production VMs without creating 200 separate alert rules. Which approach is best?
options: Create 200 individual metric alerts
Configure a single multi-resource metric alert rule scoped to the resource group with the VMs
Switch to log-based alerts only because metric alerts can't span resources
Use Application Insights availability tests
questions.explanation: Multi-resource metric alerts apply one rule to many resources of the same type, scoped by subscription, resource group, or list of resources. Per-VM alerts are unnecessary; log alerts work but are slower and pricier than metric alerts for simple thresholds.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide

questions.question: A workspace ingests 80 GB/day of audit logs that are queried only during incident response. Which configuration most reduces cost without losing the data?
options: Move all data to Basic logs and rely on archive rehydration when needed
Switch to a Commitment Tier matching the daily volume and use Archive retention for older data
Disable diagnostic settings entirely
Drop the data into a public storage account
questions.explanation: A Commitment Tier discounts predictable ingest, and Archive retention lowers per-GB storage cost for older data while keeping it searchable via rehydration. Disabling diagnostics loses data; moving to a public storage account violates security baselines.

questions.question: A Spark job over ADLS Gen2 is slow even though the dataset is only 50 GB. Investigation reveals 1.2 million tiny CSV files. What is the most effective remediation?
options: Switch the storage account to LRS replication
Compact the small files into larger Parquet files (~256 MB-1 GB) using a job before processing
Move the data to Azure Files instead of ADLS Gen2
Disable the hierarchical namespace
questions.explanation: The small-files problem is the classic ADLS performance killer — listing and opening millions of tiny files dominates runtime. Compacting them into larger Parquet (or Delta) files restores performance. Replication and disabling HNS don't address the file-count cost; Azure Files is not a big-data store.

citations.title: DP-203 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Data Lake Gen2 Layout from the DP-203 certification guide.

questions.question: A daily fact table is queried almost exclusively by `eventDate` and produces ~100 GB/day. Which partition design is best for query performance?
options: No partitioning — store all days in one folder
Partition by `eventDate` (one folder per day)
Partition by `userId` (millions of folders)
Partition by `randomGuid` to distribute writes
questions.explanation: Partitioning by `eventDate` matches the dominant filter and yields ~100 GB partitions — within the recommended 1-10 GB range when split across files. No partitioning loses pruning; user-id or random-guid partitioning creates millions of tiny partitions and destroys performance.

citations.title: DP-203 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Partitioning and File Formats from the DP-203 certification guide.

questions.question: A pipeline must copy data from an on-premises SQL Server into ADLS Gen2 over a private network. Which Integration Runtime should it use?
options: Azure IR in the same region as the storage account
Self-hosted Integration Runtime installed on a VM with line-of-sight to the source
Azure-SSIS IR running classic SSIS packages
No IR is required for SQL Server
questions.explanation: Self-hosted IR runs inside the customer network and is the only option that can reach an on-premises SQL Server over private connectivity. Azure IR runs in Microsoft's network and cannot reach private on-prem sources directly.

citations.title: DP-203 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Synapse Pipelines from the DP-203 certification guide.

questions.question: A Spark job joins a 10 GB fact DataFrame with a 50 MB dimension DataFrame and is slow because of a shuffle. What is the simplest performance fix?
options: Increase the cluster size by 4×
Broadcast the small dimension DataFrame so the join avoids the shuffle
Switch the file format from Parquet to CSV
Disable Adaptive Query Execution
questions.explanation: Broadcasting the 50 MB side ships it to every executor and turns the shuffle join into a map-side join, which is dramatically faster. Adding cluster size is brute-force; CSV is slower; disabling AQE removes a helpful optimiser.

citations.title: DP-203 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Spark Notebooks from the DP-203 certification guide.

questions.question: A Stream Analytics query needs to compute the average temperature per device over non-overlapping 1-minute windows. Which window function fits?
options: TumblingWindow(minute, 1)
HoppingWindow(minute, 1, 5)
SlidingWindow(minute, 1)
SessionWindow(minute, 5)
questions.explanation: Tumbling windows are fixed-size and non-overlapping — exactly the requirement. Hopping windows overlap; sliding windows update on every event; session windows are gap-based.

citations.title: DP-203 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Event Hubs and Stream Analytics from the DP-203 certification guide.

questions.question: A team wants the data lake to mirror Azure SQL Database changes within seconds without running nightly full-table dumps. Which approach fits?
options: Schedule a SELECT * export job every 24 hours
Enable CDC on the SQL database and stream changes through Event Hubs into a Delta sink, with idempotent MERGE upserts
Disable indexing on the source database
Run a SQL trigger that emails the data team on every change
questions.explanation: CDC + Event Hubs + Delta sink with MERGE is the canonical near-real-time mirroring pipeline. Daily exports do not satisfy 'within seconds'; disabling indexing harms the source; email triggers do not scale.

citations.title: DP-203 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Change Data Capture from the DP-203 certification guide.

questions.question: A large enterprise wants policy and RBAC inheritance to apply uniformly to all production subscriptions but not to dev/test. Which Azure construct is the right anchor?
options: A single subscription with all workloads inside
A Management Group hierarchy with separate Prod and Non-Prod groups
Resource Groups split by environment
A second Microsoft Entra tenant for production
questions.explanation: Management Groups apply Azure Policy and RBAC down through their subscriptions, so a Prod MG can carry strict policies while a Non-Prod MG carries looser ones. Resource Groups don't span subscriptions; a second tenant adds heavy identity overhead unrelated to policy.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Tenant and Subscription Design from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A platform team wants application teams to receive new subscriptions that already include shared networking, log forwarding, and policy guardrails. Which pattern best supports this?
options: Manual subscription creation with a checklist for each app team
Azure Landing Zones with automated subscription vending and CAF reference architecture
Single shared subscription with resource groups per team
Separate Microsoft Entra tenants per application
questions.explanation: Azure Landing Zones (CAF) explicitly target this scenario: subscription vending + pre-deployed platform services + policy. Manual checklists drift; a single shared subscription violates blast-radius separation; per-app tenants are excessive.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Landing Zone Patterns from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A new IoT platform ingests one million sensor readings per second and runs ad-hoc analytical queries on time-windowed slices. Which Azure data store is the best primary fit?
options: Azure SQL Database General Purpose tier
Azure Data Explorer (Kusto)
Azure Cache for Redis as system of record
Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
questions.explanation: Azure Data Explorer is purpose-built for high-ingest time-series telemetry with fast ad-hoc analytical queries. Azure SQL and PostgreSQL are OLTP engines that struggle with that ingest rate; Redis is a cache, not a system of record.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Choosing the Right Database from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A team migrating SQL Server to Azure SQL Managed Instance must keep downtime under 5 minutes. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Backup-and-restore with a maintenance window of several hours
Azure Database Migration Service in online (continuous) mode with cut-over after CDC catches up
Manual export/import using BCP
Direct disk copy of the SQL Server data files
questions.explanation: DMS online mode performs an initial seed and continuously replicates changes via CDC, allowing a near-zero downtime cut-over. Offline backup/restore requires a long window; BCP and disk copy don't sustain low-downtime.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Data Migration Strategies from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A 6-engineer team is starting a new SaaS product. They have no platform team, no service mesh expertise, and tight timelines. Which architecture is most likely to succeed first?
options: 20-service microservices on AKS with Istio from day one
A modular monolith deployed to App Service or Container Apps
A serverless functions mesh with no shared database
Three separate monoliths owned by no one in particular
questions.explanation: A 6-engineer team without platform support pays a heavy operational tax for microservices. A modular monolith delivers value faster and preserves the option to extract services later. Service-mesh microservices and a serverless mesh are over-engineered for the situation.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Microservices vs Monolith from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: An order service must publish an `OrderPlaced` event reliably whenever it commits a new order, even if the message broker is briefly unavailable. Which pattern best meets the requirement?
options: Two-phase commit between the database and the broker
Outbox pattern: persist the event to a local outbox table in the same DB transaction; a dispatcher publishes it
Fire-and-forget publish before persisting the order
Synchronously block the order API until the broker returns success
questions.explanation: The outbox pattern guarantees the event survives even when the broker is down: it is committed atomically with the order, and a dispatcher publishes asynchronously with retries. 2PC is brittle and rarely supported by modern brokers; fire-and-forget loses messages; synchronous publish couples availability.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Event-Driven Designs from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A workload requires RTO of under 60 seconds and RPO of zero across two Azure regions. Which architecture meets both?
options: Active/passive with backup/restore on failover
Pilot light DR with a 4-hour redeploy
Active/active across regions with multi-region write database (e.g., Cosmos DB) and global front door
Single-region deployment with daily backups
questions.explanation: Only active/active with a multi-region-write data layer can keep both RTO and RPO near zero. Active/passive and pilot light have minutes-to-hours RTO; single-region cannot survive a regional outage at all.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on High Availability Patterns from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A finance team wants to attribute Azure cost back to the team that owns each resource without manual reconciliation each month. What is the foundational requirement?
options: Move every team into its own subscription, regardless of size
Enforce required tags (costCenter, owner, env) at creation time via Azure Policy and use Cost Management views by tag
Disable Azure Cost Management
Switch all workloads to Spot VMs
questions.explanation: Showback hinges on consistent, enforced tags so Cost Management can split spend by owner/team. Per-team subscriptions help but are not sufficient and are heavyweight. Disabling Cost Management or forcing Spot does not solve attribution.

Drop the data into a public storage account

Deploy to a staging slot, warm it up, then swap with production
Increase the number of instances to 10 before deploying
Run the deployment from the developer machine using FTP

questions.question: A developer wants the model to always return its answer as a JSON object matching a defined schema, with no surrounding prose. What is the most reliable approach?

Place the schema description in a separate downstream service

Increase the embedding dimension to 4096
Disable the language analyzer

Long-lived feature branches that integrate quarterly

Use the azurerm Storage backend with state locking via blob lease
Commit the state file to Git and rebase frequently
Run all pipelines from a single shared developer laptop

Notify customers to clear their browser cache

questions.question: Spoke A and Spoke B both peer to a hub VNet, but VMs in Spoke A cannot reach VMs in Spoke B. What is the most common cause and fix?
options: Peering is transitive but the NSGs are blocking; remove the NSGs.
Peering is non-transitive; add a User Defined Route in each spoke that sends the other spoke's range to the hub firewall.
Spokes must always be in the same region as the hub; redeploy them.
VNet peering does not support spoke-to-spoke traffic at all under any configuration.

The privatelink.blob.core.windows.net Private DNS Zone is not linked to the consuming VNet.
Storage accounts cannot be reached over Private Endpoint.
An NSG must explicitly allow the storage public IP.

questions.question: A team needs to store 200 TB of nightly backup files that are rarely read after 30 days but must be retained for 7 years for compliance. Which configuration minimises storage cost?
options: Blob Storage in the Hot access tier with GRS replication
Blob Storage with a lifecycle policy: Hot for 30 days, then Archive tier with LRS
Azure Table Storage with row-level encryption
Azure SQL Database with TDE enabled
questions.explanation: Backups that are rarely accessed are a textbook fit for the Archive tier; LRS replication is sufficient for inert backup data. Hot tier is far more expensive for cold data; Table and SQL are not designed for large blob retention.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Azure Table and Blob Storage from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: A data engineer wants to run ad-hoc T-SQL queries directly against Parquet files in ADLS Gen2 without provisioning a warehouse or paying for idle compute. Which Synapse capability fits?
options: Dedicated SQL pool
Serverless SQL pool
Apache Spark pool
Synapse Link for Cosmos DB
questions.explanation: Serverless SQL pool charges per TB scanned and queries data-lake files directly with T-SQL — no provisioning required. Dedicated SQL pools require provisioning and incur cost while idle.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Synapse Analytics Basics from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: A Power BI report against Azure Synapse must reflect every transaction within seconds and the dataset is too large to fit in capacity memory. Which storage mode is most appropriate?
options: Import
DirectQuery
Live connection to a CSV file
Email subscription with a static PDF
questions.explanation: DirectQuery issues live queries to Synapse for every visual, so reports are always fresh and there's no in-memory cap. Import requires periodic refresh and has memory limits; CSV and PDF subscriptions don't satisfy the latency requirement.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Power BI Integration from the DP-900 certification guide.

questions.question: A large enterprise wants policy and RBAC inheritance to apply uniformly to all production subscriptions but not to dev/test. Which Azure construct is the right anchor?
options: A single subscription with all workloads inside
A Management Group hierarchy with separate Prod and Non-Prod groups
Resource Groups split by environment
A second Microsoft Entra tenant for production
questions.explanation: Management Groups apply Azure Policy and RBAC down through their subscriptions, so a Prod MG can carry strict policies while a Non-Prod MG carries looser ones. Resource Groups don't span subscriptions; a second tenant adds heavy identity overhead unrelated to policy.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Tenant and Subscription Design from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A platform team wants application teams to receive new subscriptions that already include shared networking, log forwarding, and policy guardrails. Which pattern best supports this?
options: Manual subscription creation with a checklist for each app team
Azure Landing Zones with automated subscription vending and CAF reference architecture
Single shared subscription with resource groups per team
Separate Microsoft Entra tenants per application
questions.explanation: Azure Landing Zones (CAF) explicitly target this scenario: subscription vending + pre-deployed platform services + policy. Manual checklists drift; a single shared subscription violates blast-radius separation; per-app tenants are excessive.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Landing Zone Patterns from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A new IoT platform ingests one million sensor readings per second and runs ad-hoc analytical queries on time-windowed slices. Which Azure data store is the best primary fit?
options: Azure SQL Database General Purpose tier
Azure Data Explorer (Kusto)
Azure Cache for Redis as system of record
Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
questions.explanation: Azure Data Explorer is purpose-built for high-ingest time-series telemetry with fast ad-hoc analytical queries. Azure SQL and PostgreSQL are OLTP engines that struggle with that ingest rate; Redis is a cache, not a system of record.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Choosing the Right Database from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A team migrating SQL Server to Azure SQL Managed Instance must keep downtime under 5 minutes. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Backup-and-restore with a maintenance window of several hours
Azure Database Migration Service in online (continuous) mode with cut-over after CDC catches up
Manual export/import using BCP
Direct disk copy of the SQL Server data files
questions.explanation: DMS online mode performs an initial seed and continuously replicates changes via CDC, allowing a near-zero downtime cut-over. Offline backup/restore requires a long window; BCP and disk copy don't sustain low-downtime.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Data Migration Strategies from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A 6-engineer team is starting a new SaaS product. They have no platform team, no service mesh expertise, and tight timelines. Which architecture is most likely to succeed first?
options: 20-service microservices on AKS with Istio from day one
A modular monolith deployed to App Service or Container Apps
A serverless functions mesh with no shared database
Three separate monoliths owned by no one in particular
questions.explanation: A 6-engineer team without platform support pays a heavy operational tax for microservices. A modular monolith delivers value faster and preserves the option to extract services later. Service-mesh microservices and a serverless mesh are over-engineered for the situation.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Microservices vs Monolith from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: An order service must publish an `OrderPlaced` event reliably whenever it commits a new order, even if the message broker is briefly unavailable. Which pattern best meets the requirement?
options: Two-phase commit between the database and the broker
Outbox pattern: persist the event to a local outbox table in the same DB transaction; a dispatcher publishes it
Fire-and-forget publish before persisting the order
Synchronously block the order API until the broker returns success
questions.explanation: The outbox pattern guarantees the event survives even when the broker is down: it is committed atomically with the order, and a dispatcher publishes asynchronously with retries. 2PC is brittle and rarely supported by modern brokers; fire-and-forget loses messages; synchronous publish couples availability.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Event-Driven Designs from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A workload requires RTO of under 60 seconds and RPO of zero across two Azure regions. Which architecture meets both?
options: Active/passive with backup/restore on failover
Pilot light DR with a 4-hour redeploy
Active/active across regions with multi-region write database (e.g., Cosmos DB) and global front door
Single-region deployment with daily backups
questions.explanation: Only active/active with a multi-region-write data layer can keep both RTO and RPO near zero. Active/passive and pilot light have minutes-to-hours RTO; single-region cannot survive a regional outage at all.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on High Availability Patterns from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A finance team wants to attribute Azure cost back to the team that owns each resource without manual reconciliation each month. What is the foundational requirement?
options: Move every team into its own subscription, regardless of size
Enforce required tags (costCenter, owner, env) at creation time via Azure Policy and use Cost Management views by tag
Disable Azure Cost Management
Switch all workloads to Spot VMs
questions.explanation: Showback hinges on consistent, enforced tags so Cost Management can split spend by owner/team. Per-team subscriptions help but are not sufficient and are heavyweight. Disabling Cost Management or forcing Spot does not solve attribution.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Section on Cost Modelling from the AZ-305 certification guide.

questions.question: A team wants Azure to manage the operating system, patching, and runtime so they only deploy application code. Which service model fits?
options: Platform as a Service (PaaS) such as Azure App Service
Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) such as Azure Virtual Machines
Software as a Service (SaaS) such as Microsoft 365
Bare-metal hosting in an Azure datacenter
questions.explanation: PaaS abstracts away the OS and runtime so teams focus on code. IaaS still requires patching the VM; SaaS is finished software; bare-metal is not abstracted at all.

citations.title: AZ-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: PaaS abstracts OS, runtime, and middleware so teams can focus purely on application code.

questions.question: Which workload is the best fit for IaaS (Azure Virtual Machines) rather than PaaS?
options: A legacy line-of-business application that requires specific OS-level kernel modules
A new stateless web API that scales horizontally
An email and calendaring product consumed by end users
A database fully managed by Microsoft
questions.explanation: Legacy apps with custom OS requirements need full VM control, which is IaaS. A stateless API fits PaaS; SaaS describes end-user products; a managed DB is PaaS.

citations.title: AZ-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: IaaS offers maximum control over the OS, ideal for legacy or custom workloads.

questions.question: Under Azure's shared responsibility model for a SaaS offering such as Microsoft 365, who is responsible for patching the underlying operating system?
options: The customer's IT administrators
A third-party security vendor by default
It depends on the subscription tier
Microsoft
questions.explanation: In SaaS the cloud provider owns the OS, runtime, and application. Customers retain responsibility for data and identity only.

citations.title: AZ-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: For SaaS, Microsoft manages the OS, runtime, and application; the customer manages only data and identities.

questions.question: Across every Azure service model (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), which responsibility always remains with the customer?
options: Patching the hypervisor
Physical datacenter security
Data classification and identity management
Network cabling in the datacenter
questions.explanation: Data, accounts, and access management are always the customer's responsibility regardless of service model. The other items are always Microsoft's.

citations.title: AZ-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Customers are always responsible for their data, endpoints, accounts, and access management.

questions.question: An Azure region is best described as:
options: A single datacenter building
A geographic area containing one or more datacenters with low-latency networking
A worldwide pool of all Azure capacity
A pricing tier
questions.explanation: A region is a geographic area built from one or more datacenters connected by a dedicated low-latency network. It is not a single building nor a pricing tier.

citations.title: AZ-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: A region is a set of datacenters deployed within a defined area and connected through a dedicated low-latency network.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of deploying a workload across multiple Availability Zones within a region?
options: To reduce egress data transfer cost
To comply with data residency requirements
To survive the loss of a single datacenter and meet a 99.99% VM SLA
To enable cross-region disaster recovery
questions.explanation: Availability Zones are physically separate datacenters within one region; deploying across zones protects against datacenter-level failure and unlocks the 99.99% VM SLA. Cross-region DR is a different pattern.

citations.title: AZ-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Availability Zones are physically separate datacenters within a region used for high-availability deployments such as the 99.99% VM SLA.

questions.question: Which Azure container is used to group related resources so they share lifecycle, RBAC, and tagging?
options: Management group
Resource group
Subscription
Tenant
questions.explanation: Resource groups are the smallest logical container for resources that share lifecycle and RBAC. Subscriptions and management groups sit higher in the hierarchy.

citations.title: AZ-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Resource groups are logical containers for resources that share lifecycle, RBAC, and tagging.

questions.question: An organization wants per-business-unit billing and separate quotas for engineering vs. marketing. Which Azure construct is most appropriate?
options: Two separate subscriptions, one per business unit
Two resource groups under the same subscription
Two tenants
Two regions
questions.explanation: Subscriptions are the billing and quota boundary in Azure. Resource groups do not isolate billing or quotas; tenants are an identity boundary; regions are geographic.

citations.title: AZ-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Subscriptions act as a billing and quota boundary for Azure resources.

questions.question: Which Microsoft Entra ID object represents a workload (such as an app or function) rather than a human user?
options: Service principal
Security group
Distribution list
Tenant
questions.explanation: Service principals (and managed identities) are the workload identity in Entra ID. Groups and DLs apply to users; a tenant is the directory itself.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: Service principals (and managed identities) represent workload identities in Microsoft Entra ID.

questions.question: What is the safest way for an Azure VM to call Azure Key Vault without storing any credentials in code or configuration?
options: Hard-code a service principal client secret
Use the Azure CLI on the VM with a personal account
Use a system-assigned managed identity on the VM
Use a SAS token shared across the team
questions.explanation: Managed identities issue tokens automatically without secrets in code, making them the recommended pattern for VM-to-Key-Vault access.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: Managed identities eliminate the need to store credentials in code or configuration.

questions.question: Which Entra ID feature enforces 'something you know + something you have' at sign-in?
options: Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
Conditional Access location filters
Password complexity policies
Privileged Identity Management
questions.explanation: MFA is the two-factor mechanism itself. Conditional Access can require MFA but does not implement the second factor; PIM is for just-in-time role elevation.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: Multi-factor authentication requires more than one verification factor at sign-in.

questions.question: An RBAC role assignment requires which three components?
options: Tenant, region, and resource group
Security principal, role definition, and scope
User, password, and MFA method
Subscription, tag, and policy
questions.explanation: Every Azure RBAC assignment is a triple of who (principal), what (role), and where (scope).

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: An RBAC assignment binds a security principal to a role definition at a given scope.

questions.question: You want to grant a developer permission to manage VMs in only one resource group, not the whole subscription. What is the recommended approach?
options: Assign Owner at subscription scope
Assign Contributor at the management group
Add them to the local Administrators group inside each VM
Assign Virtual Machine Contributor at the resource group scope
questions.explanation: Least privilege: pick the narrowest built-in role at the narrowest scope. The other options grant far broader access.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: Apply least-privilege using the narrowest built-in role at the narrowest scope that satisfies the requirement.

questions.question: If a user is granted Reader at the subscription scope and Owner at a resource group inside that subscription, what is their effective permission on resources in that resource group?
options: Reader (deny wins)
Custom mixed permissions
Owner (the more permissive role applies)
No access until the conflict is resolved
questions.explanation: RBAC assignments are additive; the most permissive applicable role wins unless an explicit deny assignment exists.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: RBAC role assignments are additive across scopes; the most permissive role applies unless a deny assignment exists.

questions.question: Which Azure Storage access tier is most cost-effective for blobs accessed several times per day?
options: Archive
Hot
Cool
Premium Page Blob
questions.explanation: Hot is optimized for frequent access. Cool is for infrequent access, Archive for rare retrieval, Premium for low-latency disk-style workloads.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: The Hot access tier is optimized for blobs accessed frequently.

questions.question: What is the minimum storage duration after which moving a blob to the Archive tier becomes cost-effective compared with Cool?
options: 1 day
30 days
180 days
10 years
questions.explanation: Archive has a 180-day minimum storage duration; moving data out earlier incurs an early-deletion charge that erases the savings.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: Archive blobs have a 180-day minimum storage duration and incur an early-deletion fee before that.

questions.question: Which VM power state stops billing for compute (but continues to bill for storage) on an Azure VM?
options: Stopped (deallocated)
Stopped (from inside the guest OS)
Running
Restarting
questions.explanation: Only the deallocated state releases the underlying compute. Guest-OS shutdown leaves the VM allocated, so compute billing continues.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: A VM in the Stopped (deallocated) state stops accruing compute charges; storage charges continue.

questions.question: Why are VM Scale Sets recommended over manually managing individual VMs for a horizontally scaled web tier?
options: They provide identical, autoscaling, load-balanced instances with a single configuration
They guarantee a different OS on each instance for resilience
They are free to run
They require no virtual network
questions.explanation: VMSS gives identical, autoscaling, load-balanced instances managed as one resource. The other options are false.

citations.title: AZ-104 certification guide
citations.snippet: VM Scale Sets provide identical, autoscaling, load-balanced VM instances managed as one resource.

questions.question: An enterprise needs separate billing and isolated workloads per business unit, while still applying common Azure Policy across all of them. What is the recommended structure?
options: All resources in a single subscription with tags
One subscription per business unit, all grouped under management groups for shared policy
One resource group per business unit in a shared subscription
One tenant per business unit
questions.explanation: Subscriptions provide billing and quota isolation; management groups let you apply policy across many subscriptions. Tenants are an identity boundary and rarely split per BU.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Use subscriptions for billing and quota isolation; use management groups to apply common policy across many subscriptions.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a single Microsoft Entra ID tenant for an enterprise?
options: It provides one identity boundary so users, groups, and conditional access apply across all subscriptions
It is the only way to enable Azure regions
It enforces a single Azure region for all resources
It replaces the need for RBAC
questions.explanation: A tenant is the identity boundary; one tenant lets identity and Conditional Access span all subscriptions.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: A Microsoft Entra tenant is the identity boundary that contains users, groups, and conditional access policies.

questions.question: Which Azure subscription type is best for production workloads requiring committed-use discounts and finance-managed billing?
options: Free Trial
Pay-As-You-Go developer subscription
Enterprise Agreement (EA) or Microsoft Customer Agreement (MCA)
Visual Studio Subscription
questions.explanation: EA/MCA agreements are the enterprise-grade billing constructs that unlock reservations, savings plans, and centralized invoicing. The others are dev/individual offerings.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Enterprise Agreement and Microsoft Customer Agreement subscriptions enable committed-use discounts and centralized billing.

questions.question: Which subscription in an Azure landing zone hierarchy is responsible for the hub virtual network, ExpressRoute, and shared DNS?
options: Identity
Management
Connectivity
Sandbox
questions.explanation: In the Cloud Adoption Framework, the Connectivity subscription owns hub-and-spoke networking. Identity holds domain controllers; Management holds monitoring; Sandbox is for experiments.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: The Connectivity platform subscription hosts the hub virtual network, ExpressRoute, and shared DNS for landing zones.

questions.question: What is the role of the Management management-group subscription in a CAF landing zone?
options: It centralizes Log Analytics, Azure Monitor, and policy compliance for all landing zones
It hosts every production workload
It is only used for sandbox testing
It replaces resource groups
questions.explanation: The Management subscription centralizes monitoring, logging, and compliance for the entire landing-zone estate.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: The Management platform subscription centralizes Log Analytics, Azure Monitor, and compliance reporting.

questions.question: Why are Azure Policy assignments applied at the management-group scope in a landing zone design?
options: They only apply to the management group resource itself
They cascade down to every subscription and resource beneath the group
They are required for billing
They are required to create resource groups
questions.explanation: Policy assignments inherit downward, so a single assignment at the management group enforces guardrails across every subscription beneath it.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure Policy assignments inherit from management groups down to every subscription and resource beneath them.

questions.question: A team needs a globally distributed store with single-digit-millisecond reads and multi-region writes for a session-state workload. Which Azure database service fits best?
options: Azure SQL Database single instance
Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server
Azure Blob Storage
Azure Cosmos DB
questions.explanation: Cosmos DB is the global multi-master NoSQL service. The other options are regional, relational, or unstructured.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure Cosmos DB provides global distribution with single-digit-millisecond latency and multi-region writes.

questions.question: An OLTP workload requires strong relational integrity, stored procedures, and the ability to lift-and-shift an existing SQL Server schema with minimal change. Which is the best fit?
options: Azure Synapse Analytics
Azure SQL Managed Instance
Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL
Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2
questions.explanation: Azure SQL Managed Instance gives near-100% surface compatibility with on-prem SQL Server, ideal for lift-and-shift. Synapse is for analytics; the others are not relational.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure SQL Managed Instance offers near-complete compatibility with on-premises SQL Server for lift-and-shift scenarios.

questions.question: Which Azure service is purpose-built to perform an online, near-zero-downtime migration of an on-prem SQL Server database to Azure SQL?
options: AzCopy
Azure Database Migration Service (DMS)
Azure Data Factory copy activity
Storage Explorer
questions.explanation: DMS supports online migrations with minimal downtime. AzCopy is for blob/file copies; ADF is for ETL; Storage Explorer is a GUI.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure Database Migration Service supports online migrations of SQL Server to Azure SQL with minimal downtime.

questions.question: Which Azure offering allows shipping petabyte-scale data to Azure on physical disks when network transfer would take weeks?
options: AzCopy parallel transfer
Azure File Sync
Azure ExpressRoute Direct
Azure Data Box family
questions.explanation: Azure Data Box ships physical appliances for offline data transfer. The others are network-based.

citations.title: AZ-305 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure Data Box ships physical storage devices for petabyte-scale offline transfer to Azure.

questions.question: Which Azure App Service plan tier provides VNet integration and zone redundancy suitable for production line-of-business APIs?
options: Premium v3 (Pv3)
Free (F1)
Shared (D1)
Basic (B1)
questions.explanation: Premium v3 supports VNet integration, autoscale, and zone redundancy. The lower tiers lack these production features.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: Premium v3 App Service plans add VNet integration, autoscale, and zone redundancy for production workloads.

questions.question: Which App Service feature lets you swap a 'staging' slot with 'production' atomically, including warm-up?
options: Application Insights
App Service Authentication
Deployment slots
Custom domains
questions.explanation: Deployment slots enable atomic swaps with optional warm-up, the canonical zero-downtime release pattern on App Service.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: Deployment slots provide warm-up and atomic swap for zero-downtime releases.

questions.question: Where should you store environment-specific connection strings for an App Service so they survive a slot swap without exposing them in code?
options: appsettings.json in the repo
Azure DevOps variable group only
A README.md file
App Service configuration with Key Vault references
questions.explanation: App Service application settings with Key Vault references inject secrets safely at runtime and can be marked slot-sticky. Putting secrets in code or repo is unsafe.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: App Service configuration supports Key Vault references so secrets are injected at runtime instead of stored in code.

questions.question: Which Azure Functions trigger fires when a new blob is uploaded to a storage container?
options: Blob trigger
Timer trigger
HTTP trigger
Service Bus trigger
questions.explanation: Blob triggers run on new/updated blobs. Timer is on schedule, HTTP is on request, Service Bus is on queue/topic messages.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: Blob triggers execute a function when a new or updated blob appears in the configured container.

questions.question: Which Azure Functions plan keeps a fixed number of pre-warmed instances to avoid cold starts for latency-sensitive APIs?
options: Consumption plan
Premium plan
Dedicated plan with manual scale only
App Service Free tier
questions.explanation: Premium supports pre-warmed instances to eliminate cold starts while still scaling on demand. Consumption can suffer cold starts; Free does not exist for Functions.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: The Premium plan for Functions supports pre-warmed instances to avoid cold starts.

questions.question: Which Functions trigger is best for processing a queue of messages with at-least-once delivery and dead-letter support?
options: Service Bus queue trigger
HTTP trigger
Timer trigger
Event Grid trigger
questions.explanation: Service Bus offers FIFO, sessions, and dead-letter queues. Event Grid is push pub/sub without ordering; the others do not consume queues.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: Service Bus queue triggers consume messages with at-least-once delivery, sessions, and dead-letter support.

questions.question: Which BlobClient method is appropriate for uploading a very large file using parallel block uploads in the .NET SDK?
options: Download
UploadAsync with StorageTransferOptions (parallel blocks)
OpenRead
DeleteIfExists
questions.explanation: Large uploads should use parallel block uploads tuned via StorageTransferOptions. The other methods either read or delete.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: Use BlobClient.UploadAsync with StorageTransferOptions to perform parallel block uploads for large files.

questions.question: Which token grants time-limited, scoped, signed access to a specific blob without sharing the storage account key?
options: Account access key
Bearer token from Entra app registration
Shared access signature (SAS)
Connection string
questions.explanation: SAS tokens are scoped, signed, time-limited URLs ideal for delegated access. Sharing the account key or connection string grants full control.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: Shared access signatures grant time-limited, scoped access to blobs without exposing the storage account key.

questions.question: In Cosmos DB for NoSQL, what is the most cost-effective way to query a high-volume read of items by a single field?
options: Cross-partition SELECT * with no filter
JOIN across containers
Filter inside the partition key so the query stays single-partition
Use OFFSET / LIMIT instead of WHERE
questions.explanation: Single-partition queries consume the fewest RUs; cross-partition fan-outs are expensive. Cosmos does not support JOIN across containers.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: Single-partition queries in Cosmos DB consume the fewest request units; cross-partition queries fan out and are expensive.

questions.question: Which Cosmos DB consistency level offers the lowest latency and highest availability at the cost of read order guarantees?
options: Strong
Eventual
Bounded staleness
Session
questions.explanation: Eventual is the weakest and fastest consistency. Strong is the strictest and slowest; Session is the per-client default; Bounded staleness sits in between.

citations.title: AZ-204 certification guide
citations.snippet: Eventual consistency offers the lowest latency and highest availability of Cosmos DB consistency levels.

questions.question: What is the defining characteristic of a relational database compared with a document store?
options: Data is organized in tables with a fixed schema and rows linked by keys
Data is stored as opaque binary blobs
Data is exclusively in-memory
Data cannot be queried with SQL
questions.explanation: Relational databases use tables, fixed schemas, and foreign keys. The other statements are false.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Relational databases organize data in tables with a fixed schema and rows linked by primary and foreign keys.

questions.question: What is normalization in a relational database used for?
options: Reducing data redundancy by splitting data into related tables
Increasing storage size for redundancy
Encrypting data at rest
Migrating to NoSQL
questions.explanation: Normalization removes redundancy and update anomalies. The other options describe different concerns.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Normalization reduces redundancy by splitting data into related tables.

questions.question: Which workload type is OLTP optimized for?
options: Long-running batch analytics over years of history
High-volume short transactions such as order entry
Training machine-learning models
Static website hosting
questions.explanation: OLTP handles many small, latency-sensitive transactions. OLAP handles analytics; the rest are unrelated.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: OLTP systems are optimized for many short, high-volume transactions such as order entry.

questions.question: Which Azure service is the right choice for an OLAP star-schema data warehouse with petabyte-scale analytics?
options: Azure SQL Database General Purpose
Azure Cosmos DB
Azure Synapse Analytics dedicated SQL pool
Azure Cache for Redis
questions.explanation: Synapse dedicated SQL pool is built for OLAP. Azure SQL DB General Purpose is OLTP; Cosmos is operational NoSQL; Redis is an in-memory cache.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure Synapse dedicated SQL pools are designed for petabyte-scale OLAP workloads.

questions.question: Which Azure SQL deployment option offers near 100% surface compatibility with on-premises SQL Server and supports cross-database queries and SQL Agent?
options: Azure SQL Database single database
Azure SQL Managed Instance
Azure SQL Edge
Azure Database for PostgreSQL
questions.explanation: Managed Instance gives near-complete on-prem compatibility, including SQL Agent and cross-DB queries.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure SQL Managed Instance provides near-complete compatibility with on-premises SQL Server.

questions.question: Which Azure SQL Database service tier is recommended for unpredictable, bursty workloads to optimize cost?
options: Hyperscale
Business Critical
Serverless
General Purpose Provisioned with manual scaling
questions.explanation: Serverless auto-pauses and bills per second of compute, ideal for bursty/idle workloads. The other tiers are always-on.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure SQL Serverless tier auto-pauses and bills per second, optimizing cost for bursty workloads.

questions.question: Which Azure managed database service is purpose-built for open-source PostgreSQL with high availability and zone redundancy?
options: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
Azure SQL Database
Azure Cosmos DB for MongoDB
Azure Cache for Redis
questions.explanation: Flexible Server is the recommended PostgreSQL offering with HA and zone redundancy. The others are different engines.

citations.title: DP-900 certification guide
citations.snippet: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server provides HA, zone redundancy, and managed PostgreSQL.

questions.question: A team needs MongoDB API compatibility with global distribution and SLA-backed latency. Which Azure service should they choose?
options: Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server
Azure Cosmos DB for MongoDB
